note_models.name: Obsidian-basic
Obsidian-cloze

fields: Chapter 1 Introduction > What is an OS?
A program that acts as an intermediary between user and hardware
What is virtualization?
A method that allows operating systems to run applications within other OS'es
Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Operations on Environments > When are changes in the environment produced?
At entry and exit from a block
Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Scope Rules > Where is a declaration local to a block visible?
That block and all the blocks nested within it
Chapter 4 Names and The Environment > Scope Rules > Static Scope > What is the difference between static and lexical scope?
They are two words for the same thing
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Techniques for Memory Management > Is memory management a function of a compiler or interpreter associated with an abstract machine?
Interpreter
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Techniques for Memory Management > Which functionality manages the allocation of memory for programs and for data?
Interpreter
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > When is static memory management performed and by what?
By the compiler before execution starts
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > Where does statically allocated memory objects reside?
In a fixed zone of memory which is determined by the compiler, and they remain there for the entire durectino of the program's execution.
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Static Memory Management > Are constants, object code and global variables usually statically or dynamically allocated?
Statically
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the LIFO scheme?
Last in first out.
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > A block A is entered, then a block B is entered, with the LIFO scheme, which block do we leave first?
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > A stack uses which scheme, LIFO, FIFO or FILO?
LIFO
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the memory space allocated on the stack, dedicated to an in-line block or to an activation of a procedure called?
The activation record or frame.
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What does the structure of a generic activation record for an in-line block hold?
Dynamic chain pointer, local variables and intermediate results
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What are intermediate results in an in-line block of an activation record?
Results which are only needed for something else. For example b = (a + x) / (x + y), a+x and x+y will be calculated and stored before they are divided (depending on the compiler).
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What does the dynamic chain pointer store? (recall: in-line block of a generic activation record)
A pointer to the previous activation record on the stack (or to the last activation record created).
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is a procedure?
An independent code module that fulfills some concrete task and is referenced within a larger body of source code
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > What is the difference in an activation record for a procedure and for a function?
For functions the activation record must also keep tabs on the memory location in which the function stores its return value.
Chapter 5 Memory Management > Dynamic Memory Management Using Stacks > How is the runtime management of the system stack implemented?
By code fragments which the compiler or interpreter inserts immediately before and after the call to a procedure or before the start and after the end of a block
06 Control Structures > Review Questions > Why do we need memory?
To store the state of the program
06 Control Structures > Review Questions > What kinds of data structures are we using in memory management ?
Stack and the heap
06 Control Structures > Review Questions > What does it mean that we are using a stack for memory purposes?
We use them to push activation records or frames.
06 Control Structures > Review Questions > Why do we need a stack for dynamic memory, instead of just dumping everything in memory?
We need it for recursion. With recursion we cannot allocate all possible blocks, but we can with a stack.
06 Control Structures > Review Questions > How do we implement static scoping?
We are using the static chain pointer. The compiler adds this pointer to the previous "father" block, which contains the known local variable.
06 Control Structures > Expressions > What is an expression?
A syntactic entity whose evaluation yields a value or does not end, in which case the expression is undefined. There are three main notations, infix, prefix and postfix.
06 Control Structures > Commands > What are side effects?
Changing the state of computation without returning a value
06 Control Structures > Commands > When was if then else introduced?
In ALGOL 60
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What are the two main ways that parameters are passed?
By value and by reference
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Does the bootstrap program tend to be simple or complex?
Simple
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Where is the bootstrap program usually stored?
In the computer hardware in firmware
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > What loads the kernel into memory?
The bootstrap program
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Traps (or exceptions) are another form of interrupts, and is software generated. Why are they caused?
Because of errors or by a specific request from a user program that an operating-system service be performed by executing a special operation called a system call.
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Multiprogramming and Multitasking > What does multiprogramming do?
Increases CPU utilization, as well as keeping users satisfied by organizing programs so that the CPU always has one to execute.
Chapter 1 > Operating-system operations > Multiprogramming and Multitasking > What are programs in execution called in a multiprogrammed system?
A process.
Week 6 > What is the main difficulty that a programmer must overcome in writing an operating system for a real-time environment?
Keeping the operating system within the fixed time constraints of a real-time system.
Week 6 > How does the distinction between kernel mode and user mode function as a rudimentary form of protection (security)?
It provides a form of security, in that certain instructions can not be executed unless in kernel mode. Similarly, hardware devices can only be accessed when the program is in kernel mode.
Week 6 > Some CPUs provide for more than two modes of operation. What are two possible uses of these multiple modes?
Different kinds of user modes. Specific kernel modes, for example if you want a mode to only be able to use USB

Week 6 > Give two reasons why caches are useful. What problems do they solve? What problems do they cause? If a cache can be made as large as the device for which it is caching (for instance, a cache as large as a disk), why not make it that large and eliminate the device?
Caches are useful when two or more components need to exchange data, and the components perform transfers at differing speeds.
Week 6 > How do clustered systems differ from multiprocessor systems? What is required for two machines belonging to a cluster to cooperate to provide a highly available service?
Clustered systems use messages to communicate, while processors in a multiprocessor system could communicate using shared memory
07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Why do we care about expressions?
Because we want to compute stuff
06 Control Structures > Commands > Why do we not use goto anymore?
It makes the code harder to read
08 Types > Review > How many ways of passing parameters are there?
6, Call by value, reference, result, result-value, constant and name
08 Types > Review > What are the pros of call by value?
You don't have to worry about side-effects.
08 Types > Review > What are the pro of call by reference?
You don't have to copy anything
08 Types > Review > What is the particularity of call by constant?
It's read only, otherwise it works like call by reference.
08 Types > Finally and Return in Java > What is the idea of finally?
To have a block that will be executed no matter what in a try block.
08 Types > Review > What is the difference between an exception and an error?
An Error "indicates serious problems that a reasonable application should not try to catch." An Exception "indicates conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch."
08 Types > Types > What is a type?
A collection of values (homogeneous and effectively represented (can be visualized)) equipped with a set of operations to manipulate these values.
08 Types > Types > What defines what is a type and what is not a type?
The programming language.
08 Types > Types > How many categories of types are there?
Two, scalar types (or simple types) and composite types.
08 Types > Types > What is a scalar type?
A type where values are not composed of aggregations of other values (e.g. boolean, chars).
08 Types > Types > What are composite types?
Types obtained by combining other types with appropriate constructors (e.g. string, pointer, struct).
08 Types > Types > When were enumerations introduced?
In Pascal
08 Types > Types > Are enumerations ordered or unordered?
Ordered
08 Types > Types > When were intervals (subranges) introduced?
In Pascal
08 Types > Types > Type LessThan = 0..9 is an example of which type?
Intervals
08 Types > Type equivalence > When are two types equivalent?
If each object of type T1 is also an object of type T2 and vice versa. T1 is compatible with T2 if an object of T1 can appear where a T2 would be expected.
08 Types > Type equivalence > What is type equivalence by name?
Two types are equivalent if they have the same name. Used in Pascal, Ada and Java
07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Can you do lazy evaluation if you evaluate right-to-left?
No
07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Why do we care about tail recursion?
It's faster
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > You can call by value and by reference, but what else?
Constant, Result and value-result
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by result?
Opposite of value. The value of the result is returned and modified, but is not a reference. foo(result y) {y + 1}, then y would be y + 1 from here on.
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result after the function is done running?
It is destroyed (removed from the stack)
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by value/result?
Like call by reference, but it copies the variable from outside the function to the variable inside the function and then returns when done.
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What happens to the variable inside of a function using call by result/value after the function is done running?
It is destroyed (removed from the stack)
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is the difference between call by name and call by need?
Call by name is evaluated every time it is used, while call by need is evaluated the first time it is used (Haskell has need)
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by name?
In call by name the variable is evaluated every time it is used.
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > Is deep binding used with static or dynamic scope?
Static
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is the difference between deep binding and shallow binding?

Deep binding binds the environment at the time a procedure is passed as an argument. Shallow binding binds the environment at the time a procedure is actually called. See more: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/15550648/shallow-deep-binding-what-would-this-program-print
07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is an exception?
An event which occurs during the execution of the program, that disrupts the normal flow of the programs instructions.

06 Control Structures > Commands > There are two types of iteration: {{c1::bounded}} and {{c1::unbounded}}. {{c1::Bounded}} is for numerically controlled cycles (for, do), and {{c1::unbounded}} is for logically controlled cycles (while, repeat).
07 Control Abstraction > Review from last > Commands are more in {{c1::imperative}} languages, while expressions are more in {{c1::functional}} languages
08 Types > Types > {{c3::, true}}

07 Control Abstraction > Control Abstraction > What is call by result? :: Opposite of value. The value of the result is returned and modified, but is not a reference. foo(result y) {{c1::y + 1}}, then y would be y + 1 from here on.
